Row1.Pathway ID/Name: PD‑L1 expression and PD‑1 checkpoint pathway in cancer (KEGG hsa05235)
Row1.Rationale: Durvalumab is a high‑affinity human IgG1 monoclonal antibody that blocks PD‑L1 binding to PD‑1 and CD80, restoring T‑cell recognition and cytotoxicity; its addition after concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT) in unresectable stage III NSCLC (PACIFIC paradigm) improves progression‑free and overall survival, establishing this axis as central for benefit.

Row2.Pathway ID/Name: PD‑L1 tumor proportion score (TPS)–linked immune activation
Row2.Rationale: Real‑world and retrospective PACIFIC‑style cohorts show that PD‑L1 TPS ≥1% and especially very high PD‑L1 are associated with longer PFS and OS on durvalumab, whereas PD‑L1 <1% derives little or no OS benefit, indicating that stronger PD‑L1 induction reflects a more inflamed, durvalumab‑responsive tumor microenvironment.

Row3.Rationale: In multi‑institutional stage III NSCLC cohorts treated with CRT and durvalumab, higher TMB independently correlates with prolonged disease control, likely via increased neoantigen load; in contrast, the metastatic NEPTUNE trial using blood TMB ≥20 mut/Mb to select for durvalumab+tremelimumab failed its OS endpoint, showing that bTMB alone is insufficient as a predictive biomarker in advanced disease.

Row4.Pathway ID/Name: IFN‑γ / JAK‑STAT–driven PD‑L1 induction and T‑cell–inflamed microenvironment (MSigDB HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE)
Row4.Rationale: CRT and activated T cells produce IFN‑γ, which via JAK‑STAT signaling upregulates PD‑L1 and antigen‑presentation genes; durvalumab given post‑CRT in this IFN‑γ–inflamed context sustains T‑cell effector function and mediates durable disease control in unresectable stage III NSCLC. Loss‑of‑function in IFN‑γ/JAK‑STAT pathways contributes to immune‑desert phenotypes and resistance to PD‑(L)1 blockade.

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: Antigen processing and presentation (HLA class I pathway; KEGG antigen presentation)
Row5.Rationale: Adequate neoantigen load plus intact HLA class I/B2M‑dependent presentation enable CD8 T‑cell recognition; defects in antigen presentation or “low foreignness” drive primary resistance and immune‑desert states in NSCLC despite PD‑L1 blockade, whereas CRT‑induced antigen release and preserved HLA function favor durable responses to durvalumab.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: CD8+ T‑cell infiltration / effector program (MSigDB “T CELL ACTIVATION”)
Row6.Rationale: Across NSCLC datasets, high CD8+ tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) and PD‑L1 co‑expression associate with improved outcomes to PD‑(L)1 blockade; consolidation durvalumab is most effective in tumors with pre‑existing or CRT‑induced CD8+ infiltration, reflecting a T‑cell–inflamed microenvironment amenable to checkpoint release.

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: T‑cell exhaustion and compensatory checkpoints (LAG‑3, TIM‑3, TIGIT pathways)
Row7.Rationale: Chronic antigen exposure in lung tumors induces co‑expression of PD‑1, LAG‑3, TIM‑3, and TIGIT on CD8 TILs, defining exhausted states linked to reduced ICI response; upregulation of TIM‑3 and TIGIT after PD‑(L)1 blockade represents an adaptive resistance pathway, motivating combination strategies (e.g., PD‑L1 plus TIGIT/TIM‑3 blockade) to enhance or rescue durvalumab efficacy.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR/ALK oncogenic signaling and immune exclusion (EGFR–MAPK, ALK pathways)
Row8.Rationale: Retrospective PACIFIC‑style series show markedly shorter PFS on durvalumab in EGFR‑mutant stage III NSCLC, and broader ICI data indicate limited benefit in EGFR/ALK‑driven metastatic tumors despite PD‑L1 expression, consistent with low TMB and “cold” immune microenvironments driven by these oncogenic pathways.

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: Tumor immune phenotypes: inflamed vs immune‑excluded vs immune‑desert (composite immune‑signature programs)
Row9.Rationale: NSCLC tumors with inflamed, CD8‑T‑cell–infiltrated, PD‑L1‑high microenvironments respond best to PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade, underpinning durvalumab benefit post‑CRT; immune‑excluded/desert phenotypes driven by stromal barriers, VEGF, and TGF‑β signaling show poor responses, explaining primary resistance in a subset of stage III and metastatic patients.

Row10.Pathway ID/Name: Chemoradiotherapy‑induced immunogenic modulation (CRT → antigen release and PD‑L1 upregulation)
Row10.Rationale: Definitive CRT alone yields modest long‑term survival, but adding durvalumab increases median PFS and 4–5‑year OS; CRT enhances tumor antigen release, dendritic‑cell priming, and PD‑L1 upregulation, creating a transient window in which PD‑L1 blockade consolidates and amplifies anti‑tumor immunity in unresectable stage III NSCLC.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: Immune‑related pneumonitis / lung tissue injury and repair pathways
Row11.Rationale: In durvalumab consolidation cohorts, treatment‑emergent pneumonitis—particularly early‑onset events leading to discontinuation—is associated with worse PFS and OS, suggesting that exaggerated or misdirected lung‑inflammatory pathways can curtail durvalumab exposure and undermine long‑term benefit, despite class‑typical overall safety.

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: SCLC immune contexture and transcriptional subtypes (e.g., SCLC‑I “inflamed” subtype)
Row12.Rationale: In ES‑SCLC, adding durvalumab to EP chemotherapy improves OS but with modest absolute benefit and no robust PD‑L1/TMB biomarker; emerging data suggest that an “inflamed” SCLC‑I transcriptional subtype and certain HLA class II alleles may enrich for benefit from PD‑(L)1 blockade, providing a mechanistic basis for differential durvalumab sensitivity across SCLC subtypes.
